The Donut Agency

PawLab — Email, SMS & Retention Audit

PawLab (Klaviyo account Q682D6) · pawlab.com.au · July 2026 · AUD · full order history Dec 2019 – Jun 2026 (348,504 orders · 211,800 customers) · charts are interactive — hover for exact figures

Email and SMS drive 30% of PawLab's A$9.6M year (Klaviyo last-touch, a ceiling), split evenly — 56% campaigns, 44% flows — from ~2.6 sends a week to a genuinely segmented lifecycle stack. The order data shows a slow-cycle consumable: 29.5% of customers ever buy again, on a median 123-day reorder cycle, and every entry product funnels repeat buyers toward the same hub — Stain & Odour Remover. The machinery built to work that cycle is the problem: the winback flow is live but has sent zero emails since January, the repeat-purchase flows earn A$0.22–0.32 per recipient against a A$0.82 flow baseline, and a May 2026 Gmail/Microsoft filtering event halved engaged opens before send-tightening in June produced the account's best month. Fixing the repeat-purchase layer against the 123-day cycle is the largest near-term lever. This audit reports only what the Klaviyo account and its order data show.

Part A — Email & SMS

Email + SMS attribute 30% of revenue; campaigns and flows split it almost evenly

Campaigns
A$1.64M · 56%
Flows
A$1.29M · 44%

Store revenue A$9.64M (Jul 2025 – Jun 2026, 85,968 orders, AOV A$112); Klaviyo-attributed email + SMS A$2.92M (30%, last-touch — a ceiling, not a measured contribution). The split is a reliance description, and it is balanced: neither side is starved. SMS is a real second channel — 34 campaigns and A$291K attributed, plus SMS steps inside 4 flows (A$2K).

Sends more than doubled into Dec–Apr; open rate sagged to 32%, then June's tighter sends produced the best engagement of the year

Monthly email sends vs campaign open rate · Jul 2025 – Jun 2026
Sends (left axis)Campaign open rate (right axis)

Monthly volume ran 580K–980K through winter, then spiked to 1.9M (Dec) and 1.6M (Apr) around Christmas and the rebrand/launch push. Campaign open rate drifted from ~40% down to 31.6% in April as volume scaled. In May, opens collapsed at two specific inboxes (next section); in June the program cut volume to 753K, tightened to engaged segments, and open rate jumped to 49.3% with the year's best click rate (0.90%) — while the store had its biggest month ever (A$1.17M). Click rates run 0.2–0.9% all year: the click, not the open, is the structural leak.

Bars = total email sends (Received Email, campaigns + flows). Line = recipient-weighted campaign open rate by send month (Σ open-rate × recipients ÷ Σ recipients, Donut Dashboard email_campaign_metrics, 133 campaigns). Open rate is Apple-MPP-inflated; the inflation is roughly constant month-to-month, so the trend is readable; click rate is not MPP-affected.

Deliverability: a May 2026 filtering event at Gmail and Microsoft, now recovering

The engaged-opener rate halved in one month at exactly two providers while the rest held: Gmail 43% → 24% and Hotmail/Outlook 45% → 15% (Apr → May), while BigPond/Telstra (68% → 65%), Office 365 and Apple were flat or up. Same-breadth audience both months (~123K profiles mailed), so this is consistent with an inbox-placement event at Gmail and Microsoft, not an audience change — inference; the Klaviyo deliverability score and per-send domain card are UI-only. The response is already visible in the account: sunset/exclusion segments ("Deliverability Risk / Sunset", "Soft Bounce 2X", "ED | Unengaged 30d", "ED | Marked Spam") created May 7–14, GlockApps seed lists added May 14 and Jun 8, and June's tightened sends recovered opens at both providers.

Inbox providerSends (90d)Note
Gmail1,179,909Engaged-opener rate 43% → 24% in May; recovered in June
Hotmail / Outlook924,10445% → 15% in May; recovered in June
BigPond / Telstra255,828Held 65–81% all quarter
Yahoo / AOL (Verizon)232,585Mild May dip
Apple Mail139,186MPP-inflated; steady
Office 36596,970Steady
Gsuite30,801
All other providers~137,000

DNS authentication: SPF and DKIM resolve via the Klaviyo branded sending domain (root SPF covers Google Workspace corporate mail), but DMARC is p=none with no reporting address — no enforcement and no visibility into who fails authentication on the domain — and there is no BIMI or MTA-STS. The Klaviyo DKIM selector is not externally discoverable; confirm alignment in the Klaviyo UI.

Provider rates above are monthly engaged-opener rates (unique openers ÷ unique recipients within the month, aggregates API) — valid for month-over-month and cross-provider comparison, not equal to Klaviyo's per-send "deliverability by domain" UI card, which the API cannot reproduce.

Add a DMARC reporting address now and move toward enforcement, and keep June's tightened sending discipline while Gmail/Microsoft reputation rebuilds. The May event is the account's biggest revenue risk; the June recovery shows the fix works.

List growth is steady but flat, and rests on a single popup

55,137 new email subscribers over 12 months (3.5K–6K/month) with no growth trend. Sign-ups concentrate in one form:

New email subscribers per month · last 12 months
Form (live, last 90 days)Views (unique)Sign-upsSign-up rate
First order discount – 10% Off (popup, Y7d42x)166,0207,7074.6%
Early Access – Shampoo hero form (Apr campaign, retired)6,0631,19019.6%
10% Off Pop Up – Exit form9,6423003.1%
Early Access – bottom form (retired)1,7461579.0%
Subscribe for updates (footer embed)4,589290.6%
All forms—9,383

82% of sign-ups come from the one 10%-off popup. The April shampoo-launch hero form converted at 19.6% — four times the popup — and was retired with the campaign; that mechanic (early access to a named launch) is the proven second capture path this list lacks.

Make the launch early-access form a permanent second capture path. Growth resting on one popup is a single point of failure, and the 19.6% sign-up rate is already proven on this store.

New-subscriber counts = unique profiles with a "Subscribed to Email Marketing" event per month (consent-grain, one per profile). Klaviyo's Subscriber Growth net-change report is UI-only; raw subscribe/unsubscribe event counts over-count net growth and were not used.

Mail pressure runs high — up to 13–16 emails per mailed profile in peak months

PawLab reaches most of its mailable base every month (~115–125K unique profiles), so the going-dark problem most accounts have is inverted here: the exposure is pressure. In December every mailed profile averaged 15.9 emails; in April 12.7. June's deliberate tightening cut reach to 82K profiles (~66% of April's base) at 9.2 emails each — and produced the best open, click and revenue month of the year.

MonthUnique profiles emailedTotal emailsEmails / profile
Jan 2026118,9571,140,8349.6
Feb 2026118,295903,5737.6
Mar 2026119,875913,0577.6
Apr 2026125,4371,586,84012.7
May 2026123,198773,2516.3
Jun 202682,303753,1319.2

Each square ≈ 1% of the recently-mailable base (April 2026 unique reach, 125,437); shaded ≈ 34% not mailed in June — a deliberate post-incident tightening, not lost reach. For context, 63,558 customers placed an order in the last 12 months and 20,426 in the last 90 days. A strict "emailable customers not mailed in 30/90/180d" matrix needs consent-scoped segments created in the Klaviyo UI; flagged, not estimated.

Campaigns: 2.6 sends a week to a real lifecycle segment stack — the segmentation most accounts lack is already here

133 email campaigns in 12 months (~2.6/week), averaging A$10.1K per campaign; campaign email revenue A$1.34M = 14% of store revenue. Weekly volume vs revenue, last 13 weeks:

133 campaigns~A$1.34M

Sale + Sale Last Chance + Sale Extension are 24 of 133 campaigns but A$424K — the highest revenue-per-campaign block (A$17.7K avg vs A$10.1K account avg). Product Launch is the volume engine (27 campaigns, A$212K). 72 of 133 campaigns (54%) carried an offer over the year; 21 of 43 (49%) in the last 90 days. The mix is balanced between promotional and value content rather than promo-dependent.

Segment usage — verified from each campaign's audience

Read directly from audiences.included on the 39 campaigns of the last 90 days: production sends target the ED lifecycle stack by name — "ED | Engaged Customers 30d" (23 campaigns), "ED | Engaged Prospects 30d" (21), with the At-Risk (30–90d), Lapsed, and Recent variants of Customers / 1x / Repeat / Prospects each on 6–7 campaigns, and EOFY sends to "ED | Engaged Customers/Prospects 90d". Exclusions are equally deliberate: "MC | Exclusion | International" (32), "Never Engaged" (30), "Not Subscribed" (28), and since May "Deliverability Risk / Sunset", "Soft Bounce 2X", "ED | Unengaged (30d)" and "ED | Marked Spam" (15–20 each). This is the segmentation discipline most audited accounts lack.

UsedEngagement recency × lifecycle (Prospect / 1x / Repeat customer) — carries ~all of the last 90 days' sends
RareVIP / high-AOV tiers · SMS-only audiences beyond "All SMS Contacts"
NeverProduct-category affinity (Outdoor/Turf vs Disinfectant vs Stain & Odour buyers — the axis Part B shows has 2–3× LTV spreads)

Send-side analysis (what actually receives mail); a full segment-library inventory is a separate pass. Older campaign generations resolve to the same lifecycle axis under prior segment IDs.

Flows: a wide, freshly-rebuilt stack — but the winback is live at zero sends and the repeat-purchase layer underearns 3–4×

109 flows in the account · 33 live · 65 recorded sends in the last 12 months. Flow-RPR baseline A$0.82. The stack was rebuilt Jan–Feb 2026 ("ED |" generation went live; the prior generation was set to draft), so 12-month figures blend both generations — draft rows below earned while live earlier in the window.

Coverage vs the canonical flow set

LiveWelcome · Browse abandonment (×2) · Cart abandonment — real add-to-cart trigger (×2) · Checkout abandonment (×2) · Post-purchase · Cross-sell · Repeat Buy · Review request · Shipping/transactional (Wonderment ×4) · Subscription lifecycle (Skio suite) · 30-day non-customer winback · SMS steps in 4 flows
Live but not firingCustomer Winback ("ED | PawLab | Winback Flow") — 0 sends since Jan 29 · Skio Out-of-Stock — 0 · Skio Paused — 0 · Relo Winback — 15 recipients · Subscription Reactivated — 6
Built but off (draft)"ED | PawLab | Repeat Purchase" (built Feb 17, never launched) · Ebook Welcome Series (8 sends)
MissingBack-in-stock (a "Subscribed to Back in Stock" metric exists — the signal is captured and unused) · Sunset flow (a sunset segment exists but only as a campaign exclusion, nothing works it) · replenishment timed to the 123-day cycle

Per-flow diagnostics

Where the cause sits in something the flow report can't see (trigger, filter, delay), it is flagged as a verify-in-UI inference.

  • The customer winback is live and silent. "ED | PawLab | Winback Flow" (live since Jan 29) does not appear in the 12-month flow report at all — zero recipients — while Part B shows a 123-day median reorder cycle and 148K lapsed customers. The only winback actually firing is the 30-day non-customer flow. Inference: a trigger/filter contradiction or unstarted date-trigger — verify the trigger and filters in the UI; this is the single highest-value fix in the account.
  • The 1st→2nd machine earns a quarter of baseline. "ED | PawLab | Repeat Buy" A$0.32/recipient, "ED | PawLab | Cross Sell" A$0.22, Post-Purchase Main A$0.21 — vs the A$0.82 account baseline and the old Skio billing reminder at A$14.30. These fire (86K recipients combined) but convert like broadcast, not like lifecycle mail. Inference: timing and merchandising — Part B says the customer is "due" at ~123 days and the bought-next product is almost always Stain & Odour Remover; verify each flow's delay and product blocks in the UI.
  • The rebuild left a revenue gap at the billing reminder. The draft Skio "Upcoming Order/Billing Reminder" earned A$124K at A$14.30 RPR (Jul–Feb); its ED replacement runs at A$11.35 RPR but reached only 2,369 profiles in its window. Confirm the new trigger fires for every renewal cohort, not a subset.
  • The new welcome outperforms the old. ED Welcome Flow: A$4.92/recipient vs the prior A/B welcome's A$4.41 — msg 1: 5,104 rec / A$63,157 · msg 2: 3,195 rec / A$6,986 · msg 3: 2,944 rec / A$3,857 · msg 4: 2,785 rec / A$2,362. The front emails carry it; keep, and rework the tail with objection content.
  • Abandonment coverage is genuinely complete. Real add-to-cart flows (standard + Triple Pixel) sit beside checkout flows with cross-suppression between paths — the cart/checkout naming trap most accounts fail was handled. RPRs (A$1.7–3.8) are healthy.
  • Flow graveyard: 109 flows, ~33 live. Six-plus generations (2020 PetLab originals, 2022, TT, MH, Relo, Skio, ED) sit in draft. Archive them — they add attribution noise and management overhead.

Dollar-weighted priorities

#Build / fixWhy it ranks hereSizing
1Make the live winback actually fireBuilt, live, zero sends; 148,242 lapsed customers exist and 12.1% of recent cohorts buy again in 90d unaided.lapsed-emailable × lift × A$112 [emailable share]
2Re-time the repeat layer to day ~100–123 and merchandise the Stain & Odour hubRepeat Buy / Cross Sell / Post-purchase earn A$0.21–0.32 vs A$0.82 baseline; every category's top cross-buy is the same hub product.86K recipients × (baseline − current RPR) ≈ A$45–55K/yr at parity
3Launch the built "Repeat Purchase" draft (1st→2nd)70.5% never place a 2nd order; +1pt on the 1→2 step = 2,118 customers × A$112 first reorder ≈ A$237K before downstream orders.2,118 × A$112 / pt
4Back-in-stock flowThe subscription OOS flow is dead and the BIS metric already collects intent.BIS subscribers × CR × A$112 [BIS volume]
5Automate the sunset + keep June's engaged-only disciplineProtects the Gmail/Microsoft recovery; the segments exist, nothing works them automatically.risk mitigation

What the flow report can't see: trigger configuration, filter/exclusion logic and their AND/OR operators, time delays, A/B splits, and message content (unless rendered). The winback-at-zero and low-RPR causes above need a UI pass to confirm.

Part B — Retention

29.5% come back — on a 123-day cycle no flow currently works

Order stepCustomers% of baseMedian gap
1st → 2nd62,47429.5%123 days
2nd → 3rd26,48212.5%129 days
3rd → 4th13,8476.5%120 days
4th → 5th8,2323.9%101 days

Once a customer makes the 2nd purchase, the odds keep improving: 42% of 2nd-order customers reach a 3rd, 52% of those reach a 4th, 59% a 5th. The whole game is the first repeat — and the ~4-month gap tells you exactly when the (currently silent) winback and the repeat flows should fire: pressure from day ~100, not day 30.

211,800 customers through Jun 30, 2026. Steps sequenced on distinct order days (same-day duplicates collapsed). Median gaps stable at ~4 months across steps.

Cohorts: customer value keeps climbing on first-order spend — but repeat activity is softening as the base scales

2026 Q211,701A$120————

Acquisition scaled ~10× (1,494 → ~12–13K per quarter) while 365-day LTV per customer rose from ~A$84 (2020) to a peak of A$147 (2024 Q3) — an unusually healthy pattern. But decompose it: 30-day LTV (essentially the first basket) is still climbing (A$62 → A$118), while orders per customer in year one have fallen from ~1.44 (2024 cohorts) to 1.37, and 12-month repeat activity from ~27% to 23.5% across the last mature cohorts. Value growth is coming from bigger first baskets, not more return trips — which is exactly the gap the silent repeat-purchase layer would close.

LTV = cumulative revenue per acquired customer over the window; validated against actual spend (2024 Q3: 1.41 orders × ~A$104 avg order ≈ A$147 ✓). Revenue is order net totals as synced from Klaviyo Placed Order values (top-line, pre-refund adjustments). Immature cells are blank, not zero: 365-day columns need cohorts acquired by 2025 Q2; 90-day columns by 2026 Q1. The 2026 Q2 30-day figure is slightly right-censored for June acquisitions. 2019 Q4 (1 customer) omitted.

~87% of every cohort lapses within a quarter — flat for six years, and no flow works the gap

Staggered cohort retention: each row an acquisition quarter; Q0 = the acquisition quarter (100% by definition), QN = share of the cohort placing ≥1 order N quarters later. Shaded by retention; quarters beyond Q8 flatten at ~2–4% and are omitted for width; newer cohorts have fewer observed quarters (the stagger). 211,800 customers / full order history, read 2026-07-08, capped at Jun 30.

Left-to-right, the curve is the same every year: Q1 retains 11–14%, decaying to ~5–7% by Q4 and a ~2–4% tail that keeps buying sporadically for years — that tail is unaided reactivation, no winback or replenishment fires into the >90-day gap. Down any column, cohorts are not improving: 2025 acquisition retains the same as 2021 acquisition. Six years of acquisition scaling has never moved the retention curve; the lever is a flow that works the 100–180-day lapse window, not more acquisition.

Acquisition: Outdoor/Turf and Disinfectant start the best customers; everyone's second purchase leads to the same hub

First-purchase categoryFirst-buyersAvg first orderRepeat rateRebuy same*Top cross-buy†LTV (hist.)
Stain & Odour Remover57,299A$7227.6%59%DisinfectantA$127
Disinfectant50,271A$8134.1%38%Stain & Odour RemoverA$156
Outdoor / Turf20,089A$11232.5%51%Stain & Odour RemoverA$213
Bundles19,179A$13226.6%22%Stain & Odour RemoverA$199
Accessories18,650A$12129.6%18%Stain & Odour RemoverA$200
Air / Odour Spray9,400A$11027.2%37%Stain & Odour RemoverA$163
Carpet Cleaner7,714A$9825.7%25%Stain & Odour RemoverA$147
Laundry6,938A$8439.7%45%Stain & Odour RemoverA$183

Three findings for acquisition and the welcome/post-purchase flows: Outdoor/Turf first-buyers are the most valuable (A$112 first order → A$213 historical LTV); Laundry and Disinfectant first-buyers return most (39.7% and 34.1% repeat); and the biggest entry category, Stain & Odour Remover (57K first-buyers), is also the weakest (27.6% repeat, A$127 LTV) — yet it is what everyone else's second order contains. The catalog is hub-and-spoke: acquire on turf/disinfectant problems, retain on the stain-and-odour refill.

Covers the 89% of customers whose first order maps to a Function category (account product-category map; 86% of all line items mapped). *Rebuy same = share of the category's repeat buyers whose 2nd order includes the same category. †Top cross-buy = most common different category in these first-buyers' later orders. LTV = historical revenue per first-buyer, full history.

What repeat buyers reorder: the stain-and-odour refill is the account's blade

Stain & Odour Remover
59% rebuy same
Outdoor / Turf
51% rebuy same
Laundry
45% rebuy same
Disinfectant
38% rebuy same
Air / Odour Spray
37% rebuy same
Carpet Cleaner
25% rebuy same
Bundles
22% rebuy same
Accessories
18% rebuy same
Disinfectant → Stain & Odour
49% (cross-sell)

Stain & Odour Remover behaves like a consumable refill (59% of its repeat buyers rebuy it), Outdoor/Turf and Laundry likewise. Bundles and Accessories buyers don't rebuy their entry category — they migrate to the hub. The post-purchase and repeat flows should merchandise Stain & Odour Remover to nearly every segment, timed to the ~4-month cycle.

Part C — What to do

Each move joins a retention fact to an email gap. In priority order.

  • Turn the winback on. It is built and live but has sent zero emails since January, while 70.5% of customers never reorder and the median reorder gap is 123 days. Verify the trigger/filters in the UI, fire it into the 100–180-day lapse window, and merchandise the customer's own category plus the Stain & Odour hub. Highest-value, near-zero-effort fix in the account.
  • Re-time and re-merchandise the repeat layer. Repeat Buy / Cross Sell / Post-purchase earn A$0.21–0.32 per recipient against an A$0.82 baseline. The order data says the customer is due at ~4 months and the bought-next product is Stain & Odour Remover in every category — point the flows at that, and launch the already-built "Repeat Purchase" draft. Each point added to the 1→2 step ≈ 2,118 customers × A$112 ≈ A$237K before downstream orders.
  • Protect the deliverability recovery. The May Gmail/Microsoft event halved engaged opens; June's engaged-only sending fixed it and still produced the best month ever. Keep the tightened discipline, automate the sunset segment into a flow, and add a DMARC reporting address (p=none today) so the next event is visible before it costs a month.
  • Acquire on the categories that start the best customers. Outdoor/Turf (A$213 LTV) and Disinfectant (34% repeat) outperform the biggest entry (Stain & Odour, A$127 / 27.6%). Point paid traffic and the welcome flow's product blocks at the high-LTV entries; let the hub do the retention.
  • Add the proven second capture path and the missing intent flows. The retired shampoo early-access form signed up 19.6% of viewers (4× the evergreen popup) — make launch early-access permanent. Build back-in-stock (the metric already collects subscribers) and a product-category axis in segmentation, the one axis the otherwise-excellent segment stack never uses.
Basis & method. Reports only what the Klaviyo account and its order data show. Account identity confirmed: Klaviyo account Q682D6 — PawLab (pawlab.com.au, Melbourne AU, AUD), the brand requested; a single Shopify "Placed Order" metric is the conversion metric throughout (no dual-platform split). Order-level retention computed on the full synced history Dec 2019 – Jun 2026 (348,504 orders / 212,885 customers; analysis capped at Jun 30, 2026 → 211,800 customers; order-grain dedupe verified, fan-out 1.0002; timestamps in Australia/Melbourne). The brand's history begins at launch (Jan 2020 ramp from zero), so cohorts are not left-censored. Campaign figures from the Donut Dashboard sync of this Klaviyo account (133 email campaigns, 12 months; content types from the account's classifier taxonomy verbatim); flow figures from the Klaviyo Reporting API (last-12-months window, blending the pre- and post-Feb-2026 flow generations); sends/opens/providers/forms/subscribers read live from the aggregates, forms and metrics APIs. Email/SMS revenue share is Klaviyo last-touch — a ceiling; the account's conversion-attribution window is a UI setting not exposed by the API and was not verified. Provider-level rates are window engagement (unique openers ÷ unique recipients), not per-send rates; Klaviyo's deliverability score and per-send domain card are UI-only and not reproduced. Revenue is top-line order value, not profit (no COGS); no profitability or causal claims are made. The May-2026 deliverability event and all flow-cause diagnoses are inferences from the data patterns noted and need UI confirmation.
